request.question: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?

title: Shaykh Haydar
Chullachaki
Nariphon
Skogsrå
Silili
Cura (mythology)
Thalna
Almaz (mythical beast)
Oceanus
Hulder
sentences: Shaykh Haydar or Sheikh Haydar (Persian: شیخ حیدر‎ ‎ "Shaikh Ḥaidar"; b. 1459, Amed - d. 9 July 1488, Tabasaran) was the successor of his father (Shaykh Junayd) as leader of the Safaviyya from 1460-1488.
Haydar maintained the policies and political ambitions initiated by his father.

Under Sheikh Haydar, the Safaviyya became crystallized as a political movement with an increasingly extremist heterodox Twelver Shi'i coloring and Haydar was viewed as a divine figure by his followers.

Shaykh Haydar was responsible for instructing his followers to adopt the scarlet headgear of 12 gores commemorating The Twelve Imams, which led to them being designated by the Turkish term Qizilbash "Red Head".

The Chullachaki (Quechua, "one-footed", from "chulla" or "ch'ulla" = single, odd, unpaired, asymmetric, "chaki" = foot; spelling sometimes also used in Spanish) or Chullachaqui (hispanicized spelling), also known as the Shapishico, is a mythical forest creature of the Peruvian and Brazilian Amazonian jungle.
The Nariphon (Thai: นารีผล ), also known as Makkaliphon (Thai: มักกะลีผล , from Pali "makkaliphala"), is a tree in Buddhist mythology which bears fruit in the shape of young female creatures.
The maidens grow attached by their head from the tree branches.
This tree grows at the Himaphan, a mythical forest where the female fruits are enjoyed by the Gandharvas who cut the fruits and take them away.

The skogsrå , (Forest Rå), "Skogsfrun" (The Mistress of the Forest), "Skogssnuvan", "Skogsnymfen" (Forest Nymph), "Råndan" (the Rå) or "huldra", was a mythical creature of the forest, or Rå, in Swedish folklore.
Silili is a divine figure mentioned in Tablet VI of the Epic of Gilgamesh.
Gilgamesh objects to the unwanted attractions of the goddess Ishtar, enumerating the previous lovers of Ishtar, and the misfortunes which befell them.
He claims that Ishtar once loved the horse, but ultimately decreed a number of misfortunes for him, including the continuous wailing of "his mother, Silili".
Because Silili is only mentioned in passing, there is little to say of her character, other than she is a female divine figure closely associated with the horse.
Cura is the name of a divine figure whose name means "Care" or "Concern" in Latin.
Hyginus seems to have created both the personification and story for his "Fabulae", poem 220.
In Etruscan religion and myth, Thalna was a divine figure usually regarded as a goddess of childbirth.

Determinate gender, however, is not necessarily a characteristic of Etruscan deities, and Thalna is also either depicted as male, or seems to be identified as a male figure because of the placement of names around a scene.
Her other functions include friendship and prophecy.
Her name may mean "growth, bloom."
She appears in Etruscan art in the company of Turan, Tinia, and Menrva.

The almaz (Chechen: "almaz" or "hun-sag"; Adyghe: "almesti"), roughly translated as various "feral forest-man" or "stone spirit", is a mythical beast that is considered to be an evil forest creature with magical powers residing in its hair that exists in Chechen and Ingush folk beliefs and Circassian folk beliefs.
The first "attestation" of it in writing was by a Bavarian captive of the Mongols, but it is present in the national folklore of Chechens and Ingush and of Circassians.

The male almaz is said to be hairy and hideous, and have an axe embedded in its chest, while the female is very beautiful with large breasts and golden hair, and has a "favorite pastime" of dancing naked at night under the moon.
The almaz is said to have magical powers residing in its hair, but if the hair is removed or even grabbed, it may be rendered helpless.

It has been theoried by some to have arisen under Mongolian influence, either during the Mongol invasions of Dzurdzuketia or the intervening period where the northern Dzurdzuk state of Simsir was subjugated to the Mongol-controlled Golden Horde.
The word "almaz" is a loan from Mongolian where it originally meant "forest man".
Amjad Jaimoukha however suggested that the name "almaz" may have started to have been used by North Caucasians for an already existent native concept during the sojourn of the Golden Horde of Simsir.

Oceanus ( ; Greek: Ὠκεανός "Ōkeanós", ] ), also known as Ogenus ("Ogenos", Ωγηνος) or Ogen (Ωγην), was a divine figure in classical antiquity, believed by the ancient Greeks and Romans to be the divine personification of the sea, an enormous river encircling the world.
A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.
(Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)
In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).
She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.
Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?
hop1_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Gryttie | Gryttie is a mythical creature which is reputed to dwell in the depths of Gryttjen, in Hälsingland, Sweden.  Gryttjen Lake is located fairly deep in the forest between the towns of Ljusdal and Hudiksvall.

List of Greek mythological figures | The following is a list of gods, goddesses and many other divine and semi-divine figures from Ancient Greek mythology and Ancient Greek religion.  (The list does not include creatures; for these, see List of Greek mythological creatures.)

Peder Jönsson | Peder Jönsson (died 1640), was a Swedish hunter and fisherman from Söderköping, who was executed for having confessed to sexual intercourse with a Sjörået (a lake-nymph; a mythical female creature of the lake).  Though they are other cases of the same kind in 17th-century Sweden, his was the only case were the sentence is confirmed to have been executed.

Iele | The Iele are feminine mythical creatures In Romanian mythology.  There are several differing descriptions of their characteristics.  Often they are described as virgin fairies ("zâne" in Romanian), with great seductive power over men, with magic skills and attributes similar to Nymphs, Naiads and Dryads found in Greek mythology.  They are also similar to the Samodivas in Bulgaria

Coco (folklore) | The Coco (or Cuco, Coca, Cuca, Cucuy) is a mythical ghost-monster, equivalent to the bogeyman, found in many Hispanic and Lusophone countries.  He can also be considered a Hispanic version of a bugbear, as it is a commonly used figure of speech representing an irrational or exaggerated fear.  The Coco is a male being while Coca is the female version of the mythical monster, although it is not possible to distinguish one from the other as both are the representation of the same being.

Pombero | The Pombéro is a mythical humanoid creature of small stature being from Guaraní mythology.  The legend, along with other mythological figures from the Guaraní, is an important part of the culture in the region spanning from northeast Argentina northward through the whole of Paraguay and southern Brazil.  The Pombéro is said to capture particularly ungrateful girls, and force them to kiss him, and later, force them to have sexual intercourse with him.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "A hulder is a seductive forest creature" — passage [1].
- Swedish names: "skogsrå 'forest spirit'" and "Tallemaja 'pine tree Mary'" — passage [1].
- Explicit linkage: "originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda." — passage [1].

2) Ambiguities:
- Multiple name variants appear: huldra (Norwegian), skogsrå, Tallemaja (Swedish), and ulda (Sámi) — all in passage [1].
- The passage mentions two candidate divine figures, "Huld" and "Holda"; it does not make clear which is the canonical match (or whether both are equivalent).

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entities as written in the passage: "völva divine figure Huld" and "the German Holda."
- Missing fact needed to answer the question: an authoritative confirmation (from the Huld and/or Holda pages) that the hulder is "originally the same being as" which divine figure — i.e., a clear statement on the Huld and/or Holda page linking that divine figure to the hulder (and any context on equivalence between Huld and Holda).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Huld" page: check lede/infobox and etymology/Origins sections for "völva," links to hulder, and wording that confirms "same being."
  Search keywords: Huld völva hulder; open page and check lede/etymology.
- Open "Holda" (or "Frau Holle"/"Holda") page: check lede/folklore sections for mentions of hulder or Huld and statements of equivalence.
  Search keywords: Holda Frau Holle hulder connection.
- Optionally open "Skogsrå" and "Tallemaja" pages (Swedish names) to see local identifications and cross-references to Huld/Holda.
- Optionally open "Völva" page to confirm the nature of that divine figure and typical identifications.

Do not provide the final answer yet — retrieve those pages/sections to confirm which divine figure is the correct link.
hop2_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Huld | In Scandinavian mythology, Huld is only referenced by "völva" or "seiðkona", that is a woman who practiced the seiðr.  She is mentioned in the "Ynglinga saga", "Sturlunga saga" and a late medieval Icelandic tale.  In the latter source, she is Odin's mistress and the mother of the demi-goddesses Þorgerðr and Irpa.  As her name suggests, Huld may be in origin the same being as the Huldra and the German Holda.

Holda | In Germanic legends, Frau Holda (or Frau Holle) was the protectress of agriculture and women's crafts.  Holda, Holle, Huld, and Hulda may be cognate of the Scandinavian creature known as the "huldra".  Jacob Grimm made an attempt to establish her as a Germanic goddess.
872 Holda | 872 Holda is a minor planet orbiting the Sun.
Mary Holda | Mary Holda [Elrod] was an utility infielder who played in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League.  She batted and threw right-handed.

Coat of arms of Lardal | The coat of arms of Lardal was approved on 17 July 1992 and is a gold hulder on a red field.  The flag was at the same time approved as a yellow hulder on a red field.  Both the coat of arms and flag were designed by the artist Arvid Sveen.

Hafdís Huld | Hafdís Huld Þrastardóttir (born 22 May 1979), known simply as Hafdís Huld, is an Icelandic singer and actress.  She began her musical career as a member of the electronic band GusGus in 1995 and left the group in 1999.  Hafdís made her solo debut with her 2006 album "Dirty Paper Cup".
response.summary_2: A hulder is a seductive forest creature. Huld is a völva (a seiðkona who practised seiðr) in Scandinavian mythology, and the passages say she may in origin be the same being as the Huldra and the German Holda. Holda (Frau Holda) is a Germanic figure associated with agriculture and women's crafts, and the passages state she may be cognate with the huldra.
response.answer: the völva Huld (and the German Holda)
